title: Trees & Trie
learning_outcomes: Describe structural characteristics of Trees & Trie.
Derive mathematical and programmatic bounds for Binary Tree traversals (DFS recursive/iterative vs BFS), Trie insert/search/delete, Trie autocomplete prefix trees.

content_sections.title: Trees & Trie
content_sections.content:
### Asymptotics, Structures & Graph Algorithms
Data structures and algorithms optimize operations across time and space complexities.

#### Floyd's Cycle Detection Proof
To find if a linked list has a cycle, we use a slow pointer $S$ (speed 1) and fast pointer $F$ (speed 2). If a cycle exists, they will meet. Let the distance from head to cycle entrance be $A$, and the distance from entrance to meeting point be $B$. The remaining cycle distance is $C$. At collision, $F$ has traveled double the distance of $S$:
$$2(A + B) = A + B + k(B + C) \implies A + B = k(B + C)$$
$$A = (k - 1)(B + C) + C$$
If we reset one pointer to the list head and advance both pointers at speed 1, they will meet exactly at the cycle entrance after $A$ steps.

#### $O(N)$ Heap Construction
Inserting elements one by one takes $O(N \log N)$. Building a heap from an array using `sink()` (bottom-up heapify) takes $O(N)$ because the maximum sink path of nodes at height $h$ is $h$, and the number of nodes at height $h$ is $\frac{N}{2^{h+1}}$:
$$\sum_{h=0}^{\log N} h \frac{N}{2^{h+1}} = \frac{N}{2} \sum_{h=0}^{\log N} \frac{h}{2^h} = O(N)$$

#### Dynamic Programming Space Optimization
For the 0/1 Knapsack problem, the traditional state is $dp[i][w]$. Since row $i$ only references row $i-1$, we can compress memory to a single row array $dp[w]$ by iterating $w$ backwards from capacity $W$ to 0, preventing overwriting current values.

### Asymptotic and Spatial Complexity Analysis
We summarize the computational characteristics for Trees & Trie implementations in the following reference matrix:

| Process/Operation | Worst-Case Time | Average-Case Time | Space Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Core Logic Execution | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Memory Operations | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(N)$ |
